decks.label: Loop2 Opener - Jab & Step
decks.notes: Fresh 5-card warrior deck a player has by loop 2. All 1-stamina, 1.2cd attacks (Jab=9, Quickstep=4x2) so it never stalls against the trivial mult-1.0 early enemies; passive +1 stamina/4.5s easily sustains 1-cost spam. Guard last as a cheap panic block. STR multiplier already boosts the flat 9s.

decks.label: Loop2 Aggro - All Blades
decks.notes: Slightly greedier loop2 build leaning on the two best cheap T2s a player might find early: Flurry Step (1s, 4+4dex) and Reckless Strike (1s, 9 + rage, minor self-bleed). All 1-stamina/<=1.2cd, front-loaded T1 Jab/Quickstep to open, T2 in mid slots. No expensive cards to skip on empty stamina.

decks.label: Loop5 Tempo Slasher
decks.notes: 7-card loop5 deck (approxL5, STR ~3-4). Opens on cheap 1s Jab/Quickstep, then 1s T2 staples (Flurry Step, Reckless Strike, Stormstrike haste). Bloodprice Strike (12 + rage, costs 4 self-HP) is the closer in the last slot - it's a 1s card so it rarely skips, and the HP cost is fine vs mult-1.0 normals. Cost stays at 1s avg so stamina never bottoms out.

decks.label: Loop5 Two-Hit Spike
decks.notes: 6-card variant that splashes the 2-stamina Quickstrike (8dex) for a bigger single hit. Quickstrike is placed AFTER two 1s openers so stamina has recharged before the engine reaches it - avoids an early skip. Hollow Echo (vengeance 5->8) closes. DEX is low early so this is slightly weaker than the all-1s build; included as a realistic 'found a 2-cost' deck.

decks.label: Loop8 Physical Core
decks.notes: 9-card loop8 deck introducing the first T3s. Pinprick Volley (1s, 5dex pierce x3) is cheap enough to interleave mid-deck; Bloodlash Salvo (2s, 9 + permanent +4 STR this combat) is the late-slot scaler that snowballs the back half. Front 5 are all 1s/1.2-1.4cd to guarantee a clean open. Avg cost ~1.2s, sustainable.

decks.label: Loop8 Earthcleaver Bruiser
decks.notes: 9-card bruiser leaning on big single-target earth-physical hits: Earthcleaver (18 pierce) and Quarry Dance (10dex, +8 if armor>=10). Pure attacks fill the front for tempo; the two 2s T3 hits are spaced apart (slots 6 & 8) with 1s cards between so stamina recharges and neither skips. Strong vs the higher-HP loop8 normals where pierce matters.

decks.label: Boss1 doom_knight Slayer
decks.notes: 10-card loop-10 deck for the doom_knight headline check (380HP, def3, 9dmg). Bloodlash Salvo early (slot5) stacks +4 STR per cast to power the big single hits later; Mountain's Will (20str) and Earthcleaver (18 pierce) punch through def3. Pure-attack and pierce beat a defensive boss better than armor builds. Front 4 are 1s openers; 2s hits are interleaved with 1s cards so the engine never stalls on stamina vs a long boss fight.

decks.label: Boss1 Sustained Pierce
decks.notes: Alt boss1 build emphasizing Pinprick Volley (cheap pierce that ignores doom_knight's def3) and Cleaver's Tax (12str pierce, doubles to 32 with 5+ rage from Reckless/Bloodprice). Rage is fed by Reckless Strike + Bloodprice + Cleaver's combo. 1s openers front-loaded; the lone 2s detonators sit mid/late so stamina is available. Tests whether the pierce+rage line clears the boss at ~0.8 winrate.

decks.label: Loop15 STR Snowball
decks.notes: 12-card mostly-T3 deck for loop15 (mult 1.1, approxL9, STR ~6). Two Bloodlash Salvo-style scalers up front (Bloodlash slot5) build STR before the heavy hitters land. Mostly-T3 with two efficient T2 staples (Flurry Step, Reckless Strike) kept for cheap tempo and rage. Heavy 2s hits are separated by 1s cards (Pinprick, the T1s) so the deck keeps flowing despite slow regen across the longer fight.

decks.label: Loop15 AoE Multistrike
decks.notes: 12-card DEX/multi-hit variant (Triple Slash 6dex x3 AoE, Pinprick Volley pierce x3, Quickstep Sigil 5dex x3 + haste). Haste from Sigil/Stormstrike speeds the whole engine, raising effective DPS. Cheap Pinprick (1s) and the T1s front the deck; the 2s triple-hitters sit mid where stamina is full. Good for the multi-enemy loop15 battery; relies on DEX which is still modest, so a touch weaker single-target than the STR build.

decks.label: Boss3 lizard_king Executioner
decks.notes: 14-card endgame-ish physical deck for boss3 (lizard_king, bossMult 1.1, approxL13, STR ~9). Bloodlash Salvo slot5 + repeated big hits exploit the high STR multiplier. Pierce density (Mountain's Will, Earthcleaver, Cleaver's Tax, Pinprick) handles boss armor/def. Rage fed by Reckless/Bloodprice/Cleaver. 1s openers front, heavy 2s hits interleaved with 1s cards so a 14-card deck still cycles without stamina starvation over a long boss fight.

decks.label: Boss3 Pure-Attack Highroll
decks.notes: 13-card tuned single-target line for boss3. All-attack/counter/earth, zero magic - maximizes the STR global multiplier on a deck of flat high-damage hits. Bloodlash (slot5) and Bloodprice rage feed Cleaver's Tax's 32-pierce payoff. Granitewrath (14 + veng 10 pierce) and Mountain's Will add burst. Front 4 are 1s; the dense 2s block in slots 5-9 is the one realism risk, mitigated by the 50 stamina pool and 1s cards seeded between - tests how close to a stall a pure-2s-heavy endgame deck runs.

decks.label: Loop-10 Bleed/Rage Opener vs Doom Knight
decks.notes: Realistic loop-10 deck (~L6, STR~6). Opens with all-1s stamina engine: Reckless Strike (9+3rage+self-bleed) and Sidestep&Slash (gives +1 stam back) keep stamina topped while seeding bleed and rage; Bloodprice Strike is the burst+rage spike. Razor Stance up early so every later hit re-applies bleed (multiplying the n(n+1) DoT). Bulwark Vow gives a small armor cushion + Brace rage vs the knight's 9-dmg swings. T3 payoffs sit late: Vein Splitter (bleed multiplier) and Berserker's Ledger (24 raw str) close the 380HP; Crimson Spiral only fires once 5+ rage is banked, which the engine has by then. Stamina-only and front-loaded so it never stalls.

decks.label: Loop-10 Aggro Rage Burst vs Doom Knight
decks.notes: Leaner, more burst-forward variant. Jab + Reckless Strike + Bloodprice Strike + Sidestep ramp rage fast (Reckless +3, Bloodprice +2) so Cleaver's Tax (12 pierce, +20 pierce at 5+ rage) lands as a real burst into the knight's def 3. Iron Reckoning gives a touch of armor AND turns banked rage into +dmg-per-rage on attacks. Fewer cards (8) is plausible for a fresh loop-10 forge; risk is less sustain, so the self-bleed from Reckless/Berserker is a real cost - tests whether the rage payoffs out-race the boss before self-bleed + boss damage tips it over.

decks.label: Loop-15 Bleed Engine (post-boss1)
decks.notes: Loop-15 (~L9, STR~7, bossKills 1 so normals at 1.1x). Mature bleed package: Razor Stance aura early so the cheap 1s attacks (Reckless, Sidestep, Quickstep) each tack on bleed. Quicksilver Bleed adds dex this combat, sharpening every later dex-scaled bleed (Razor Cadence, Vein Splitter). Order puts cheap engine first, then the 2s T3 finishers once stamina/rage are built. Crimson Spiral last as the rage-gated nuke. 11 cards, mostly T3 with efficient T2 staples - a coherent build a player who's been forging counter/attack/agility cards would actually run.

decks.label: Loop-15 Rage-Threshold Control
decks.notes: Rage-centric sub-variant leaning on the Fury threshold payoffs. Wrathshell Vow (exhaust, +6 rage + 1/2s) and Wrath Squall (exhaust, +rage on HP loss) are the rage batteries; they sit mid-deck so the cheap 1s rage-gen (Reckless +3, Bloodprice +2, Bulwark Brace +3) has already banked some Fury before they amplify it. Cleaver's Tax and Crimson Spiral both gate on 5+ rage, so this build deliberately over-invests in rage to keep both firing. Bulwark Vow + Iron Reckoning give the armor floor warriors lean on. Tests whether the rage threshold gates reliably stay satisfied through a fight at 1.1x.

decks.label: Loop-20 Bleed/Rage vs Iron Golem
decks.notes: Loop-20 (~L10, STR~7-8). Iron Golem has a defense-affinity armor ratchet, which is the key test: bleed is a DoT that (per C5) is NOT armor-reduced once applied, so this build wants to STACK bleed fast and let it tick through the golem's armor rather than trade raw hits into it. Razor Stance + Vein Splitter + Razor Cadence pile bleed to the 6-stack cap quickly; Cleaver's Tax and Crimson Spiral provide PIERCE bursts that ignore the golem's armor for the finish. 12 cards, cheap engine front-loaded. This is the headline 'can bleed beat an armor boss' matchup.

decks.label: Loop-20 Pierce-Heavy Rage vs Iron Golem
decks.notes: Anti-armor sub-variant that prioritizes PIERCE + rage gates over raw attacks, since the golem's armor blunts non-pierce damage. Wrathshell Vow front-loads Fury so Cleaver's Tax (+20 pierce) and Crimson Spiral (24 pierce) are online; these two pierce nukes plus stacked bleed DoT are the win condition. Iron Reckoning's per-rage damage bonus and Bulwark Vow's armor keep the warrior alive through the golem's hits. Order banks rage before the gated payoffs. Diagnoses whether pierce + DoT is enough vs the armor ratchet or whether the bleed payoffs need a buff at this stage.

decks.label: Loop-30 Optimized Bleed vs Lizard King
decks.notes: Loop-30 (~L13, STR~9, normals 1.2x). Tuned bleed build. Engine order: Reckless + Sidestep + Razor Stance aura first to seed bleed and dex, then Quicksilver Bleed banks +dex this combat, sharpening every dex-scaled bleed that follows (Razor Cadence, Vein Splitter). Crimson Cascade adds a bleed spreader. Vein Splitter is the centerpiece - its 'if enemy has bleed, +1 bleed per hit' explodes once Razor Stance/Quicksilver have primed the target. Wrathshell Vow + the rage gates (Crimson Spiral, Cleaver's Tax) provide the burst. 13 cards near forge cap. Tests bleed's ceiling against a tankier boss.

decks.label: Loop-30 Rage-Burst vs Lizard King
decks.notes: Rage-forward sub-variant for loop-30. Doubles down on Fury generation: Reckless (+3) and Bloodprice (+2) up front, then both rage batteries (Wrathshell Vow, Wrath Squall) so the 5+rage gates stay green for Cleaver's Tax, Crimson Spiral, and Wrath Brand (rage -> 12x2 + burn). Iron Reckoning converts the big banked Fury into per-hit damage. This is the rage-payoff stress test: at high stacks the Fury flat-damage (cap 12 pre-STR) plus the gated pierce nukes should out-pace the lizard king. Cheap engine first, exhaust batteries mid, gated nukes late.

decks.label: Loop-40 Endgame Bleed Engine vs Bog Witch
decks.notes: Loop-40 (~L15, STR~10, normals 1.3x). Near-cap (14) endgame bleed build. Same proven order: cheap 1s engine + Razor Stance aura seed bleed/dex, Quicksilver banks dex, then the dex-scaled T3 bleeds (Razor Cadence, Vein Splitter) and Crimson Cascade snowball the DoT to the 6-cap with the higher INT/DEX of L15. Rage batteries (Wrathshell Vow) feed the gated pierce nukes (Cleaver's Tax, Crimson Spiral) for armor-ignoring burst. Bulwark Vow + Iron Reckoning + Wrathshell give the survival floor needed against bog_witch at 1.15x boss scaling. This is the lane's deepest realistic build.

decks.label: Loop-40 Hybrid Bleed+Rage+Burn vs Bog Witch
decks.notes: Hybrid sub-variant splashing the bleed->burn cross-synergy cards (Kindle Strike: +3 burn if enemy bleeding; Cinderscar: bleed-per-burn; Wrath Brand: rage->dmg+burn). All stamina-based so it fits the warrior 50-stamina pool with no mana strain. Plan: Reckless/Sidestep seed bleed, then Kindle/Cinderscar layer burn on the bleeding target for a second DoT axis, while the rage gates (Cleaver's Tax, Crimson Spiral) and Wrath Brand burst. Tests whether the bleed+burn keyword bridges (Kindle/Cinderscar) pull their weight or are off-curve weak vs a single-axis pure-bleed list at the same stage.

decks.label: Boss1 Shield-Bash Convert (loop10 vs doom_knight)
decks.notes: Realistic loop-10 warrior shell. Front three slots are cheap 1s armor builders (Guard, Parrying Stance which refunds stamina, Bramble Bulwark) so by slot 4 armor is already 20+ and Shield Bash (damage=armor) hits hard. Granite Lunge and Thornwall keep refilling armor between the two Shield Bash casts; Body Slam Vow at slot 8 is the T3 pierce finisher that also rebuilds armor. All cards are 1s except the one T3, so the 1.2-1.6s CDs stay affordable on warrior's 50 stamina + regen. Jab tail is a cheap filler so the deck never stalls if armor is briefly spent.

decks.label: Boss1 Aegis Spike (loop10 vs doom_knight)
decks.notes: Pure-defense opener that leans on the single defense->damage finisher line. Two Guards plus Parrying/Bulwark Vow stack armor fast and cheaply (all 1s). Aegis of Returning Wrath (slot 6, 2s) is the headline: 22 armor + 6 pierce scaling per 4 armor, so it fires off an already-tall stack AND keeps the wall up against doom_knight's 9 dmg. Granite Lunge and Body Slam Vow are the secondary armor->damage converters. Jab is cheap insurance so a stamina dip never stalls the array.

decks.label: Boss2 Mountain's Answer Wall (loop20 vs iron_golem)
decks.notes: Iron golem has a defense-affinity armor ratchet, so EVERY finisher here is Pierce. Cheap 1s builders open (Guard, Parrying, Bramble, Thornwall, Guard) to clear the armor>=32 gate. Reforge Vow (slot 6, +50% armor gain aura) supercharges the subsequent big gains so Aegis (slot7) and Mountain's Answer (slot8, 26 armor + 22 pierce if armor>=32) both detonate near max. Body Slam Vow and Shield Bash are the recurring pierce converters that rebuild and re-spend. Reforge Vow is the only 1m card; the rest is stamina, staying inside warrior's 30 mana / 50 stamina.

decks.label: Boss2 Salvo Tempo (loop20 vs iron_golem)
decks.notes: Faster, lower-variance variant that converts armor into raw repeating hits instead of one alpha. Phalanx Drift (slot6) gives a 22-armor cushion plus haste so the back half cycles quicker. Bulwark Salvo (slot7, deal 6 twice +1 per 2 armor) and Body Slam Vow are the recurring finishers; Shield Bash and double Granite Lunge keep armor topped between casts. All Pierce-or-flat physical so the iron-golem armor affinity doesn't eat the damage. Entirely stamina, very sustainable on 1.2-2.4s CDs.

decks.label: Boss3 Reforge Citadel (loop30 vs lizard_king)
decks.notes: Mature loop-30 engine. Reforge Vow goes in early (slot5) on purpose so its +50% armor multiplier is live before the big builders, inflating Aegis and Mountain's Answer to massive pierce numbers. Cheap 1s wall in front guarantees the armor>=32 gate is met by the time Mountain's Answer is reached. Body Slam Vow / Shield Bash / Bulwark Salvo form a recurring spend-and-rebuild loop so the deck keeps converting after the alpha. Mostly stamina + a single 1m on Reforge Vow.

decks.label: Boss3 Citadel Inferno Alpha (loop30 vs lizard_king)
decks.notes: Tests the spend_armor detonator finisher. The whole front is a deep cheap-armor ramp (2 Guards, Parrying, Bulwark Vow, Bramble, Thornwall) with Reforge Vow boosting the gains, so by slot 8-9 armor is very tall. Aegis spikes once, then Citadel Inferno (slot9, 2s+1m, exhaust) consumes the ENTIRE armor stack for 2 pierce each — a one-shot alpha. Because Citadel empties armor and exhausts, Body Slam Vow at slot 11 rebuilds a fresh wall to survive the rest. Plausible mid-game build that probes whether the exhaust-detonator is worth a slot vs the repeatable converters.

decks.label: Boss4 Pierce Rush vs Poison (loop40 vs bog_witch)
decks.notes: Bog witch deals poison, which ignores armor, so this build is tuned to CLOSE FAST rather than turtle indefinitely. Reforge Vow + Aegis + Mountain's Answer assemble a huge pierce burst early-mid, and the recurring trio (Body Slam Vow, Shield Bash, Bulwark Salvo) sustains pressure so the fight ends before poison stacks become lethal. Double Jab tail keeps tempo flowing while armor regenerates. All-stamina except one 1m on Reforge Vow.

decks.label: Boss4 Sustain Wall (loop40 vs bog_witch)
decks.notes: Adds a little self-heal/recovery against poison via Flowstrike (slot4: 10 dmg + 10 armor + haste) and Last Stand Bulwark (slot11) which spikes 12 pierce + extra armor when below 50% HP — directly relevant to bog_witch's grind. Otherwise the same armor->pierce core (Reforge, Aegis, Mountain's Answer, Body Slam Vow). The haste from Flowstrike speeds the whole back half so the finishers cycle faster against the poison clock. Pure stamina + one 1m Reforge Vow.

decks.label: Boss5 Endgame Tank-Buster (loop50 vs desert_golem)
decks.notes: Optimized endgame armor engine vs a high-HP tank. Reforge Vow (slot5) is placed before the heavy builders so its +50% multiplier inflates everything after it. The wall (Guard x2, Parrying, Bramble, Thornwall) easily clears Mountain's Answer's armor>=32 gate, and Aegis + Mountain's Answer deliver two huge pierce alphas. Body Slam Vow / Bulwark Salvo / Shield Bash / Granite Lunge are the repeatable converters that keep grinding desert_golem's large HP pool after the alphas. Almost all 1s/2s stamina with a single 1m, sustainable at L16.

decks.label: Boss5 Citadel + Aegis Double Alpha (loop50 vs desert_golem)
decks.notes: Maximal armor ramp feeding two big spenders back-to-back: Aegis (slot8) and Mountain's Answer (slot9) spike off the Reforge-boosted wall, then Citadel Inferno (slot10) consumes whatever armor remains for a final 2-pierce-per-armor nuke against the desert_golem's huge HP. Because Citadel exhausts and empties armor, Body Slam Vow (slot11) rebuilds a survival wall afterward. This is the highest-ceiling armor-finisher line in the pool and stress-tests whether stacked spend_armor finishers can out-race an endgame tank. One 1m (Reforge) plus Citadel's 2s+1m; rest stamina.

decks.label: Loop 15 — Stoneguard Bruiser
decks.notes: Survival-first armor stack vs loop15 normals (mult 1.1, ~L9). Opens with cheap 1s armor: Guard then Parrying Stance (regens 1 stam) then Granite Lunge (armor + scaling dmg) so the deck never stalls on stamina. Bulwark Vow + Bramble Bulwark keep armor topped; Shield Bash (dmg=armor) is the early payoff once a few layers are up. T3 tail is the win condition: Body Slam Vow (1s, pierce scales with armor), Bedrock Bulwark (Brace), then Concussive Smash for the slow/stun control beat, capped by Mountain's Answer (26 armor → 22 pierce when armor>=32) and Mountain's Will. All stamina, avg cost ~1.3s — sustainable. Control (Concussive) sits mid so it lands before the big armor finishers detonate.

decks.label: Loop 15 — Slow/Stun Wall
decks.notes: Control-leaning armor wall: leans on earth/air physical slow+stun to throttle the loop15 battery while armor stacks. Cheap 1s open (Guard, Parrying Stance, Tremor Dash) keeps tempo. Stoneward Reprisal (8 armor + Brace stun) and Dustward (16 armor + Brace slow) are the control anchors; Galekick/Tempest Pike apply slow on attacks; Concussive Smash is the hard stun + slow swing. Phalanx Drift (22 armor + Brace dmg) and Mountain's Answer close. Pure stamina, low avg cost; the slow/stun mid-deck blunts the +10% normal mult so HP cushion stays high.

decks.label: Boss 2 — Iron Golem Grind (armor finisher)
decks.notes: vs iron_golem (400 HP, def 4, 10 dmg, +shield ratchet). High def + self-shield punishes small hits, so this deck builds a huge armor pool and converts it via the allowed armor->damage line. Cheap 1s armor opens and survives the 10-dmg cadence. Pierce matters: Body Slam Vow, Aegis of Returning Wrath (22 armor, 6 pierce + per-4-armor), Mountain's Answer (22 pierce) and Mountain's Will all bypass the def/shield. Concussive Smash stuns to deny the golem's attack tempo while armor builds. Citadel Inferno (2s+1m, exhaust) is the dump-the-bank finisher that consumes the whole armor pool into pierce — the single sanctioned armor->damage convert. 1m splash only on the closer so mana never bottlenecks the warrior pool.

decks.label: Boss 2 — Iron Golem Stun-Lock
decks.notes: Control answer to the iron_golem shield ratchet: keep it stunned/slowed so its shield interval and 10-dmg hits land less, buying time for armor-gated pierce to grind 400 HP. Stoneward Reprisal Brace-stun + Concussive Smash (2 stun + 3 slow) + Dustward Brace-slow form the control core; Tempest Pike/Tremor Dash feed slow. Win condition is Mountain's Will (20 dmg, +8 pierce at armor>=20) and Mountain's Answer pierce, both fueled by the armor the wall already holds. Pure stamina, opens cheap. Slightly lower raw burst than boss2-1 but trades for a safer profile against the shield enemy.

decks.label: Boss 3 — Lizard King Counter-Wall
decks.notes: vs lizard_king (370 HP, def 3, 10 dmg multi-hit ×2, enrage <30%). The two-hit attack triggers Brace/Vengeance windows constantly, so counter+defense cards overperform. Thornwall and Reforge Vow (+50% armor for 12s) inflate the pool fast against the double-tap. Tombplate (18 armor + sub-40% HP bonus armor + rage) is the panic button for the enrage phase. Armor-gated pierce (Aegis, Body Slam Vow, Mountain's Answer/Will) is the win condition through def 3. Concussive Smash stuns to skip an enrage tick. Citadel Inferno dumps the inflated pool as pierce to close before enrage gets dangerous. Cheap 1s open keeps it from stalling.

decks.label: Boss 3 — Lizard King Stun Control
decks.notes: Control-max line vs lizard_king's multi-hit + enrage: stack slow/stun to gut its effective DPS, especially through the 1.5x enrage speed. Stoneward Reprisal + Concussive Smash + Dustward chain stun/slow; Tempest Pike feeds slow. Armor base (Guard, Parrying Stance, Bulwark Vow, Phalanx Drift) survives the double-tap. Mountain's Will/Answer pierce closes through def 3. Tectonic Reckoning (3s+2m, exhaust, 3 stun + 50 pierce) is the elite single-shot finisher in the last slot — by then leveling + relics afford the spike, and it can erase the enrage phase outright. One heavy mana splash only on that capstone.

decks.label: Boss 4 — Bog Witch Burst-Through-Drain
decks.notes: vs bog_witch (410 HP, def 2, 7 dmg, drain heals 15%, multi-hit). The 15% lifesteal means a slow armor grind risks an unkillable loop, so this build pairs the armor wall with high pierce burst to out-pace the heal. Def is only 2, so big single hits land hard: Mountain's Will (20 + 8 pierce), Cliffwind Maul (12 + 10 pierce at armor>=12), Mountain's Answer (22 pierce), Aegis. Concussive Smash stuns to deny attack ticks (each denied attack is a denied drain). Citadel Inferno is the alpha-strike closer dumping the armor pool as pierce — burst is exactly what beats a drain enemy. Cheap 1s armor opens; armor is up before the finishers fire so the gates/scalers are online.

decks.label: Boss 4 — Bog Witch Lockdown Wall
decks.notes: Lockdown variant vs bog_witch: the witch's drain only triggers on her attacks, so heavy stun/slow starves her healing while armor-gated pierce chews the 410 HP. Stoneward Reprisal Brace-stun, Concussive Smash (2 stun + 3 slow), Dustward Brace-slow, Tempest Pike slow — chain control to freeze her cadence. Armor wall (Guard, Parrying Stance, Bedrock Bulwark, Phalanx Drift) absorbs the 7-dmg multi-hit. Mountain's Will/Answer pierce through def 2 is the kill, and Tectonic Reckoning (exhaust, 3 stun + 50 pierce) is the capstone that both locks and bursts. Pure stamina until the final mana splash; opens cheap and stays sustainable.

decks.label: Mixed Bruiser — loop5 starter (attack core + burn splash)
decks.notes: Fresh ~6-card loop5 deck a real warrior would have. Reckless Strike leads (1s, cd1.2, top-OPS) and self-feeds rage; Jab is a free-flowing 1s filler so the deck never stalls on empty stamina. Bloodprice Strike (12, +1 stam) and Sidestep & Slash (bleed splash, +1 stam) both REFUND stamina so the early loop keeps tempo on 50 max stamina. Kindle Strike is the burn splash (cheap 1s). One Guard at the tail for a panic block — placed last so it only fires when the cheaper attacks are on CD. Average cost ~1s = sustainable vs +1/4.5s regen.

decks.label: Mixed Bruiser — loop5 (agility core + bleed splash)
decks.notes: Leaner 5-card agility-flavored variant. Reckless Strike + Quickstep + Flurry Step are all 1s/cd1.2 spam that opens reliably every turn; Sidestep & Slash adds the bleed splash and a stam refund. Guard tail for the one defense card. Tiny deck = cards recycle fast, so DEX scaling on the agility hits matters; intentionally lighter to model a player who drafted few cards by loop5.

decks.label: Mixed Bruiser — loop8 (physical core + burn + 2 defense)
decks.notes: 9-card loop8 deck: first real T3 (Cinder Thrust, 7 twice + 2 burn) appears mid-list after the cheap 1s spam has primed stamina. Front five are all 1s cd~1.2-1.6 reliable openers with two stam-refunds (Bloodprice, Sidestep). Quickstrike (2s) is placed after the refunders so 2-stamina is available. Burn splash = Kindle Strike + Cinder Thrust. Two defense cards: Shield Bash (turns incidental armor into damage) and Guard. Interleaves resource use; nothing stacks 2s back-to-back.

decks.label: Mixed Bruiser — loop8 (bleed-lean splash)
decks.notes: Same loop8 power band but the splash is bleed instead of burn. Razor Stance (t2-counter-counter) lays a bleed-on-hit aura, then the physical hits stack it; Vein-style T3 not yet drafted so Sidestep + the aura carry bleed. Cheap 1s spam front-loaded, the lone 2s card (Quickstrike) sits late. Two defense cards (Guard + Bulwark Vow which also feeds rage). Models the 'I drafted counter cards' player.

decks.label: Mixed Bruiser — boss1 vs doom_knight (burn splash)
decks.notes: The headline loop10 realism deck vs doom_knight (380HP/def3/9dmg). 10 cards, a couple T3. doom_knight has def 3, so the cheap 9-damage Jab loses a chunk to armor — but Reckless/Bloodprice/Quickstrike hit hard enough to overcome it, and burn (Kindle + Cinder Thrust) bypasses def via DoT. Bloodlash Salvo gains +4 STR/combat so later turns ramp into the boss. Order: cheap reliable openers first to build tempo + rage, then T3 payoffs once stamina/STR are up, defense tail for the 9-dmg swings. Should sit ~0.8 winRate with real HP cost.

decks.label: Mixed Bruiser — boss1 vs doom_knight (armor-lean bruiser)
decks.notes: Tankier boss1 line for players who drafted more defense. Flowstrike (10 dmg + 10 armor + haste) and Shield Bash convert armor into pressure (the one allowed armor-as-tempo angle, not a conversion engine). Front-loaded cheap stam attacks build rage + STR; Bloodlash Salvo ramps. Bulwark Vow at the tail gives armor+rage when topping up. More survivable but slower TTK than boss1-1 — tests whether the bruiser can grind the boss without racing.

decks.label: Mixed Bruiser — loop15 (T3 physical + burn splash + defense)
decks.notes: 12-card post-first-boss deck (normalMult 1.1). Now mostly T3 with two efficient T2 staples (Reckless Strike, Bloodprice). Triple Slash (cd1.6, 6×3) and Bloodlash Salvo (STR ramp) are the workhorses; Cinder Thrust + Wickfencer + Kindle keep the burn splash. Granitewrath (14 + vengeance 10) is the late single-target finisher. Order keeps the cheap 1s openers up front so the 2s T3 always has stamina by the time it comes up; only one 2s card runs before the first stam-refund clears.

decks.label: Mixed Bruiser — loop15 (bleed splash bruiser)
decks.notes: Bleed-splash flavor of the loop15 bruiser. Vein Splitter (t3-agility-attack-counter, the highest-OPS bleed) is the centerpiece, fed by Sidestep, Razor Stance aura, and the physical hits. Still a true bruiser: Triple Slash + Bloodlash + Granitewrath are the bulk damage; bleed is the splash that finishes. Cheap reliable front, 2s cards spaced, Guard tail.

decks.label: Mixed Bruiser — boss2 vs iron_golem
decks.notes: vs iron_golem (loop20, defense-affinity armor ratchet). PIERCE matters here: Mountain's Will (t3-attack-earth-earth, 20 + 8 Pierce at 20 armor) and Granitewrath's vengeance Pierce punch through the golem's stacking armor. Burn (Cinder Thrust + Kindle) is DoT that bypasses armor entirely — key vs an armor boss. Bloodlash Salvo ramps STR so the late hits crack the ratchet. Cheap openers build rage/STR first; Flowstrike + Shield Bash give incidental armor + a panic block tail.

decks.label: Mixed Bruiser — boss3 vs lizard_king
decks.notes: boss3 (loop30, bossMult 1.1, normalMult 1.2). 12 cards, heavy T3 with two T2 staples. Mixed win conditions: Triple Slash + Mountain's Will for burst, Vein Splitter for a bleed finish, Cinder Thrust burn splash for DoT pressure, Bloodlash Salvo to ramp STR across the longer boss fight. Front two cheap openers prime stamina and rage; the 2s T3 block is back-loaded but each is cd<=2.4 so they cycle. Flowstrike + Shield Bash provide the defense layer to survive the bigger boss swings.

decks.label: Mixed Bruiser — boss5 vs desert_golem (endgame tuned)
decks.notes: boss5 (loop50, bossMult 1.2, normalMult 1.4) — a tuned 12-card endgame bruiser. Bloodlash Salvo placed early to start the +4 STR/combat ramp ASAP (compounds across a long fight). Core burst: Triple Slash, Mountain's Will, Granitewrath; Vein Splitter bleed + Cinder Thrust burn as the splash that scales with the now-high STR/DEX. Two armor-tempo cards (Flowstrike, Stormhilt) keep the hero alive through the harder swings while still dealing damage. Reckless Strike + Jab stay as cheap stamina-recycling glue.

decks.label: Mixed Bruiser — boss6 vs infernal_dragon (endgame tuned, full 15)
decks.notes: Full 15-card forge-cap endgame deck for the deepest requested stage (loop60, bossMult 1.25, normalMult 1.5). Bloodlash Salvo front-loaded for max STR ramp; then the heavy T3 damage suite (Triple Slash, Vein Splitter bleed, Mountain's Will, Granitewrath) plus a burn splash (Cinder Thrust, Wickfencer). Three+ defense cards (Flowstrike, Stormhilt, Shield Bash, Bulwark Vow, Guard) tail the list because at this depth the dragon's hits demand a survival layer — but they double as armor-as-tempo (Shield Bash / Flowstrike) so the defense isn't dead damage. Cheap 1s glue (Reckless, Jab, Bloodprice) keeps stamina flowing under the heavy 2s T3 load.

decks.label: Messy Drafted Warrior (loop8, mixed)
decks.notes: A casual loop-8 warrior who kept too many starter cards and a couple of dead-weight picks. Front three slots (Jab, Quickstep, Reckless Strike) are cheap reliable 1s stamina that always opens the fight; Quickstrike (2s) follows as the real damage. Then the build degrades: Guard (filler armor), Crimson Tithe (peakOPS 3.75, near-dead resource gen), Riposte (aura, no direct damage), Bulwark Vow (low-OPS armor), and free Gust at the tail. Average cost is sustainable (mostly 1s) so it won't fully stall, but the back half is wasted tempo — this is the suboptimal-but-functional curve a real player limps in with.

decks.label: Off-Pool Splash Warrior (loop8, stalls on mana)
decks.notes: A messier variant: the player picked up several MANA cards despite the warrior's tiny 30-mana pool. Slots 1-3 (Jab, Quickstrike, Quickstep) are stamina and open fine. But Mend (mana heal, off-role for a warrior), Quake (mana slow) and Kindle Strike (stamina but pure burn-setup, no payoff card) drag tempo; the player can't sustain back-to-back mana spends with only +1 mana/4.5s, so those slots intermittently skip. Bloodprice Strike (good) and the agility cards are buried late where the engine may stall before reaching them. Demonstrates how off-pool splashes stall a fixed-order deck.

decks.label: Messy Warrior vs Doom Knight (boss1, T1/T2 grind)
decks.notes: The headline realism check: a genuinely weak loop-10 warrior deck against doom_knight (380 HP, def 3, 6 dmg, enrages at 50%). Front-loaded cheap stamina (Jab, Quickstep, Reckless Strike, Quickstrike, Bloodprice Strike) gives a reliable opening burst that can grind 380 HP past def 3. The back half is dead weight (Guard, Bulwark Vow, Riposte aura, Crimson Tithe, free Gust) that contributes little but doesn't actively stall since it's cheap. This is the test: can a sloppy build still scrape a win on the boss that is SUPPOSED to be easy? It should clear, but with a thin HP cushion.

decks.label: Defensive Hoarder vs Doom Knight (boss1, over-armored)
decks.notes: A common casual mistake: over-drafting armor and survival cards with too little offense. Jab and Quickstep open, then the deck piles armor (Guard, Bulwark Vow, Parrying Stance, Shield Bash, Iron Reckoning). Shield Bash and Granite-style armor-spend give SOME damage, but the kill clock is slow against 380 HP and the enrage at 50% punishes the long grind. This deck tests whether a survival-tilted weak build can still close the boss before timing out (C3 says armor is intentionally race-losing) - expect a low win rate or timeouts, illustrating the failure mode of too-defensive drafting.

decks.label: Bare-Bones Starter vs Doom Knight (boss1, undersized)
decks.notes: An undersized 7-card deck from a player who barely drafted at loop 10. It's mostly clean cheap stamina attacks (Jab, Quickstep, Reckless Strike, Quickstrike) which cycle quickly in fixed order, so DPS is actually OK per-card, but the thin deck means a high share of plays are the two dead slots (Riposte aura, Bulwark Vow armor). Tests the floor: even a tiny, partly-dead warrior deck should be able to out-DPS doom_knight's 6 dmg/2.5s with reliable 9-12 damage attacks, though the cushion will be slim and variance high across repeats.

decks.label: Boss1 Poison Ramp + Single Detonator (doom_knight)
decks.notes: Loop-10 poison list a real player would have: opens with two cheap 1m appliers (Mire Bloom front + a copy later) plus Slipstream/Stormsplash/Mireglide so the deck reliably fires on mana while passive regen (1m/4.5s) keeps up. Mireglide+Alchemic Drain bake in armor/heal to survive doom_knight's race (poison is race-losing per C3). Slipvenom Tempo (1s+1m) is the stack engine; Steaming Plague stacks the DoT into the quadratic zone; Marsh Squall is the lone detonator placed late to cash the built pool for the kill on the 380HP/def3 boss. Order interleaves 1m and 1s+1m so it never stalls on one resource.

decks.label: Boss1 Catalyst Burst (double-then-detonate)
decks.notes: Tighter 9-card variant leaning on Bog Catalyst (double poison) right after Slipvenom Tempo to spike the stack count, then Marsh Squall to detonate. Cheap mana appliers (Mire Bloom, Slipstream, Mireglide) precede the dual-resource cards so mana/stamina are banked before the both-cost catalysts and detonator fire. Tests whether the double->detonate line out-paces doom_knight without heavy sustain.

decks.label: Loop15 Poison Volume Clear (normals x1.1)
decks.notes: 12-card mid deck for the normal battery: heavy on cheap 1m appliers (Mire Bloom x2, Slipstream, Stormsplash, Mireglide, Alchemic Drain, agility-fire-water) so quadratic DoT ramps on every short enemy and the deck never stalls. Slipvenom Tempo + Bog Catalyst + Steaming Plague accelerate stacks; Brine Bedrock adds heal-on-vengeance sustain across the loop. One Marsh Squall closes the rare beefy normal. Detonator is single because normals die to DoT alone.

decks.label: Loop15 Sustain Poison (long battery survival)
decks.notes: 11-card variant trading a detonator for extra armor/heal appliers (Mireglide x2, Alchemic Drain x2, Brine Bedrock) so the hero survives the x1.1 multi-enemy loop on flat values while poison does the killing. Front loads the cheapest mana cards; the two 1s+1m catalysts sit mid-deck after stamina has banked. No exhaust cards, so it can grind a long battery without losing tools.

decks.label: Boss2 Pierce Poison (iron_golem armor ratchet)
decks.notes: 13-card loop-20 list vs iron_golem, whose defense affinity ratchets armor — poison DoT ignores armor and the two detonators (Drowning Lance + Marsh Squall) deal Pierce, so this is the right counter. Build order: cheap mana appliers first (Mire Bloom, Slipstream, Stormsplash, Mireglide) to stack while regen banks, then Slipvenom Tempo + Bog Catalyst (double) + Steaming Plague push poison deep into the quadratic band, then Drowning Lance and Marsh Squall consume the pool for big Pierce bursts. Brine Bedrock + Alchemic Drain heal to outlast the golem.

decks.label: Boss2 Double-Detonate Spike
decks.notes: 12-card variant that runs two Bog Catalyst and two Slipvenom Tempo to reach a huge poison pool fast, then double-detonates (Drowning Lance + Marsh Squall) for two Pierce spikes that bypass iron_golem's armor in one window. Cheap appliers up front keep it from stalling; the both-cost catalysts and detonators are spaced so stamina recovers between them. Less sustain than variant 1 — tests pure burst against the boss.

decks.label: Boss3 Optimized Poison Engine (lizard_king)
decks.notes: 14-card loop-30 list, mostly T3. Two Slipvenom Tempo and two Bog Catalyst give the fastest realistic ramp to a high poison pool; Steaming Plague tops it; both detonators (Drowning Lance + Marsh Squall) cash for the kill against lizard_king's bigger HP at x1.1. Cheap 1m appliers (Mire Bloom, Slipstream, Stormsplash, Mireglide, agility-fire-water) front the deck so it opens reliably and the dual-resource catalysts/detonators fire only after resources bank. Alchemic Drain + Brine Bedrock keep the long race survivable.

decks.label: Boss3 Control-Poison (slow lockdown + DoT)
decks.notes: 13-card variant splashing Stormsplash x2 and a cheap Quake (1m slow) so the deck throttles lizard_king's attack speed (slow soft-control, C5) while quadratic poison kills — buying time the race-losing DoT plan needs. Cheap mana cards dominate the early slots; Slipvenom Tempo + Bog Catalyst build the pool and Marsh Squall detonates once. Heal appliers sustain through the slowed boss.

decks.label: Boss4 Endgame Tuned Poison (bog_witch)
decks.notes: Full 15-card forge-cap endgame poison list for loop-40 bog_witch (x1.15). Tuned engine: 2x Slipvenom Tempo + 2x Bog Catalyst + Steaming Plague drive poison into the quadratic ceiling fast; both detonators (Drowning Lance + Marsh Squall) provide two Pierce burst windows for the high-HP boss. Cheap 1m appliers (Mire Bloom, Slipstream, Stormsplash, Mireglide x2, Alchemic Drain, agility-fire-water) front-load so the opener is guaranteed and the dual-resource cards never brick on empty resource; armor/heal from Mireglide/Alchemic Drain/Brine Bedrock sustains the longest race in the lane.

decks.label: Boss4 Sustain-Heavy Poison (attrition)
decks.notes: 15-card attrition variant for bog_witch: maximizes heal/armor poison appliers (Mireglide x2, Alchemic Drain x2, Brine Bedrock x2, defense-water-water heal+poison) so the hero outlasts the boss while quadratic DoT and two Slipvenom Tempo grind it down. Single detonator (Marsh Squall) plus Bog Catalyst double provide the finishing spike. Cheap mana cards open; the both-cost catalysts/detonator are placed after stamina banks. Tests whether sustain-poison can win the long race where the burst list might die first.

decks.label: Burn Bank-and-Cash vs Doom Knight
decks.notes: Loop-10 mage (~L6, INT~3). Opens with cheap 1m appliers (Spark, Flame Dart) to bank burn while mana is fresh; free Gust for tempo. Pyre (1m) cashes+rebanks mid-fight against the 380HP boss. Cinder Sprint (1m self-detonator) is the early cheap nuke; the 1s+1m Cinderlance sits last so the small stamina pool has regenerated for the big cash. Magma Vein adds armor cushion vs the 9-dmg hits. Order interleaves so nothing stalls on empty mana back-to-back.

decks.label: Spark-Pyre Tempo (lean, no detonator stall)
decks.notes: Pure mana-only build (no stamina detonators) so it never skips on an empty stamina bar — a realistic player who hasn't drafted Supernova/Cinderlance yet. Two Pyres act as repeating consume+rebank engines, Cinder Sprint as the mana detonator. Steady 1m cadence stays inside the +1 mana/4.5s regen most of the fight. Tests whether pure self-detonation alone can close doom_knight.

decks.label: Firestorm Bank → Supernova Cash (loop15 normals)
decks.notes: Loop15 (~L9), normals at 1.1x. Firestorm (1m aura: 10 burn if 3+ cards) supercharges the bank once the cheap appliers have warmed up, so it's slotted 3rd after two openers. Cinder Sprint mid as a cheap rolling cash; Supernova (2s+1m exhaust) is the AoE finisher and sits late so both a burn pool and a little stamina exist; Cinderlance closes single targets. Magma Vein armor buys time across multiple normal fights.

decks.label: Double-Detonator Burn (Pyre engine + Cinder Sprint loops)
decks.notes: All-mana, no exhaust, no stamina cards — the most economy-stable loop15 build, leans on repeatable cashers instead of one big bomb. Two Pyres + two Cinder Sprints recycle the burn pool over and over; three Flame Darts + two Sparks keep the pool topped. Firestorm last as a passive bank-booster. A genuine player's mid-game burn deck before they find Supernova; tests sustained-cash DPS against the soft-cap.

decks.label: Burn vs Iron Golem (armor-pierce reliant)
decks.notes: Loop20 (~L10) vs iron_golem with its defense-affinity armor ratchet. Burn DoT ignores armor entirely (key vs golem), and the two pierce detonators (Supernova, Cinderlance) punch the big cash through armor. Firestorm front-loaded to keep the pool fat so each cash is large. Cinder Sprint for a rolling mid cash. This is the stress test of whether burn beats the armor-stacking boss without any armor-pierce on the DoT itself.

decks.label: Pyre-Surge Amplified Burn (build-around)
decks.notes: Uses Pyre Surge (1s+1m) to DOUBLE the banked burn and add +2-per-tick before cashing — slotted after the bank is built (6th) so there's a real pool to double. Cinder Sprint then Supernova cash the inflated pool. Tests the T3 burn amplifier line as a build-around payoff vs the soft-cap; the doubling matters most right before a detonator, so the order front-loads appliers then amp then cash.

decks.label: Mature Burn Engine vs Lizard King
decks.notes: Loop30 (~L13). Near-cap 13 cards, mostly T3 with efficient T2 staples (Flame Dart, Pyre, Firestorm). Bank chain: Spark/Flame Dart openers → Firestorm → Pyre Surge double → Cinder Sprint rolling cash → Supernova + Cinderlance big pierce cashes. Magma Welling (2s) adds 8 burn + 14 armor as a stamina-funded bank+cushion late once stamina has pooled. Two Pyres re-bank between cashes so detonators always have fuel.

decks.label: Sustain Burn (Steam Surge survival splash)
decks.notes: Same burn core but splashes Steam Surge (1m: deal 6, +4 if burn, heal 4) for the bigger lizard_king bite — a realistic player keeping a self-heal in a still-mostly-mana shell. Steam Surge sits 3rd so its 'enemy has burn' bonus is live after the first applier. Tests whether trading one detonator slot for sustain still closes boss3 or whether burn must go all-in on cash.

decks.label: Endgame Burn vs Bog Witch (full 15)
decks.notes: Loop40 (~L15) full forge-cap deck, the tuned endgame burn list. Quench Lance (T3, 1s+1m) gives an INT ramp when 10+ burn is consumed, helping the low-INT mage's later cashes scale. Firestorm + Pyre Surge + dual Pyre keep an enormous pool; Cinder Sprint rolls cash while Supernova/Cinderlance deliver the pierce burst that the witch's HP needs. Heavy 2-cost cards (Supernova, Magma Welling, Cinderlance) are spaced apart so the mage's small stamina bar refills between them.

decks.label: Mixed-DoT Tremor Detonate variant
decks.notes: Burn-first deck that also includes Tremor Detonate (2s, consumes burn+bleed+poison for str-pierce) as a second bomb — Cinderscar's vengeance-bleed feeds it a little extra stack fuel. Tests whether the all-DoT detonator is competitive with the pure-burn Supernova for a mage whose STR is low (Tremor scales on STR, not INT). Stamina bombs (Tremor, Cinderlance) are split apart and placed late so the 30-stamina mage can actually afford both across the long boss4 fight.

decks.label: Loop 8 Boil & Burn (mana DoT opener)
decks.notes: Loop8 mage at ~L6, INT low so leans on flat DoT volume. Front slots are the cheapest, lowest-CD pure-mana appliers (Spark 1m/1.2, Flame Dart 1m/1.2) so the deck always opens and stacks burn fast; free Gust gives tempo. Pyre (slot4) is the burn engine: it consumes the early burn pool then re-applies, so it wants burn already on target. Mend + Steam Surge are cheap sustain that also rewards the burn on the enemy (+4). Boilstep is the signature 1m hybrid (burn+poison) that bridges into the poison plan; Alchemic Drain (1m) and Mire Bloom (1m) layer poison + small heal. All pure-mana or single-element so a 60-mana pool sustains them; no stamina stall.

decks.label: Loop 8 Steaming Plague snowball
decks.notes: A poison-leaning variant. Cheap burn (Spark, Flame Dart) opens and chip-burns while Alchemic Drain (1m, slot3) seeds the first poison. Steaming Plague (slot5) snowballs once poison is present (+2 extra). Pyre keeps a burn pool alive for the hybrid. Bog Catalyst (1s+1m, slot7) doubles the accumulated poison for a big back-half spike — placed late on purpose so there is poison to double. Single stamina-coster (Bog Catalyst) keeps the shallow 30 stamina from stalling; Mend backstops HP.

decks.label: Boss1 doom_knight DoT race
decks.notes: Loop10 average vs doom_knight (380HP/def3). DoT pierces def, so a poison+burn mage should race it. Open cheap (Spark/Flame Dart, 1m each) to bank burn; Pyre (slot3) is the first detonation + reseed. Boilstep + Alchemic Drain layer poison while burn ticks. Steaming Plague (slot7) snowballs the poison pool, then Supernova (slot8, 2s+1m exhaust) cashes the whole burn pool as pierce for the finisher. Supernova sits late by design (needs a big burn pool first) and is the only heavy 2s+1m card so the stamina pool isn't drained early. Mend/Steam Surge added for the HP cushion the boss demands.

decks.label: Boss1 burn-detonator focus
decks.notes: Heavier burn-detonator line for the boss. Two Spark-tier openers + Flame Dart build burn quickly; Pyre is the recurring engine. Pyre Surge (slot6, 1s+1m) doubles burn and amps +2/tick right before Supernova (slot7) detonates the doubled pool as pierce — the deck's burst window. Quench Lance grants INT-on-big-consume to make the magic scaling matter for a low-INT mage. Single dual-resource detonator pair (Pyre Surge + Supernova) is the only stamina draw; cheap pure-mana fillers keep the engine fed between them.

decks.label: Loop15 hybrid DoT engine
decks.notes: Post-boss1 (normalMult 1.1), ~L9, deck grown to 12. Cheap mana appliers still lead so it opens reliably. Mid-deck is the dual-DoT core: Boilstep (burn+poison), Alchemic Drain (poison+heal), Steaming Plague (poison snowball), Bog Catalyst (double poison). Two detonators close: Supernova for burn, with Pyre Surge as the burn amplifier just before it. Detonators and Bog Catalyst (the only stamina costs) sit in back slots so the deep mana engine runs the early/mid turns and stamina isn't requested until it has regenerated.

decks.label: Loop15 poison-primary, burn splash
decks.notes: Poison-primary build that splashes burn for the detonator payoff. Alchemic Drain + Boilstep seed poison early; Steaming Plague and Bog Catalyst snowball/double it; Venom Dance (1s) and Slipstream (1m) add stacks with pierce damage. Marsh Squall (slot9, 2s exhaust) is the poison detonator that cashes the doubled pool as pierce. Spark/Flame Dart provide the burn that Boilstep/Steam Surge reward. Venom Dance + Marsh Squall are the only stamina cards and sit mid/late so the mana engine never stalls early.

decks.label: Boss2 iron_golem armor-bypass DoT
decks.notes: iron_golem (loop20, bossMult1.05) ratchets armor, so DoT (which bypasses armor) is the right tool. Open cheap to stack burn+poison through the armor; Boilstep/Steaming Plague/Bog Catalyst build a huge poison pool. Two detonators: Marsh Squall (poison pierce) and Supernova (burn pierce) cash the pools as the armor-piercing finishers. Quench Lance feeds INT for the low-INT mage's late scaling. Both heavy detonators sit in the back third so the early mana engine establishes the pools first; only Marsh Squall (2s) and Supernova (2s+1m) tap stamina, spaced apart to avoid a stall.

decks.label: Boss3 lizard_king full DoT engine
decks.notes: Loop30 (~L13, normalMult1.2) vs lizard_king. Mature 14-card dual-DoT engine, mostly T3 with cheap T1/T2 staples for tempo. Early mana appliers open and bank both DoTs; the mid is the full poison/burn core (Boilstep, Alchemic Drain, Steaming Plague, Bog Catalyst, Quench Lance, Pyre Surge). Two detonators close it: Marsh Squall (poison) and Supernova (burn), each fired after its pool is large. Venom Dance adds late poison stacks. Stamina is only requested by the two exhaust detonators and Bog Catalyst, all back-loaded, so the 30-stamina pool regenerates enough between them; the 60-mana pool carries the bulk.

decks.label: Boss5 desert_golem optimized dual-DoT
decks.notes: Loop50 endgame (~L16, normalMult1.4, desert_golem bossMult1.2). Tuned 15-card (forge cap) dual-DoT engine. Lead with the cheapest reliable appliers (Spark, Flame Dart, Boilstep) to bank both DoTs through the boss's bulk; Bog Catalyst + Steaming Plague multiply the poison pool while Pyre/Pyre Surge/Quench Lance run the burn engine and feed INT. Marsh Squall and Supernova are the twin pierce detonators, placed in the final third so they fire on maxed pools. Venom Dance + Slipstream top off poison stacks just before detonation. Only the two exhaust detonators and Bog Catalyst cost stamina, spread across late slots; everything else is mana so the deep pool sustains the long boss race without stalling.

decks.label: Boss1 Mage Slow/Stun — Quake + Sandfury win-con
decks.notes: Loop-10 mage control vs doom_knight. Opens with cheap 1m Quake (4 slow) to immediately throttle the boss's 2.5s cd, then Frostbind for stun + armor and Tremor Lock to extend the lock once stunned. Spark/Gust/Mend are cheap free/1m tempo fillers that never stall the mana economy. Sandfury (2m) is the win-con: 8x2 + 2 pierce/slow, paying off the slow stacks Quake builds. Card order front-loads the 1m/free control so the deck reliably opens and keeps the boss frozen while INT-weak flat damage chips it down; Sandfury and Cinder Squall sit mid/late where mana has regenerated. Average cost is sustainable on 60 mana + passive regen.

decks.label: Boss1 Mage Stun-Lock — Concussive Smash splash
decks.notes: Variant leaning on a stamina splash for a hard hit. Quake + Frostbind + Tremor Lock front load the slow/stun engine on cheap mana. Concussive Smash (2s) is the one stamina splash — deal 14 + 2 stun + 3 slow — affordable on a mage's 30 stamina once and a big tempo swing that both stuns and adds the slow Cinder Squall later detonates. Mend/Gust keep tempo. This tests whether a mage can splash a single heavy stamina control card to clear doom_knight without overspending its small stamina pool.

decks.label: Loop15 Mage Slow Engine — Sandfury + Thunderstrike
decks.notes: Post-boss1 (1.1x normals) bigger 12-card mage control deck. Two copies of Quake bookend the deck to keep a steady slow supply. Frostbind + Tremor Lock + Bedrock Snare form the stun-conversion chain (Tremor Lock and Bedrock Snare only pay off once a stun lands, so they sit AFTER Frostbind). Cinder Squall and Sandfury are the dual win-cons that turn accumulated slow into damage; Thunderstrike Catalyst (2s) is the late slow-detonator finisher that dumps all slow stacks for 6/stack pierce. Cheap 1m/free cards lead so the deck opens reliably; the heavier 1s+1m / 2s cards trail where resources have regenerated.

decks.label: Loop15 Mage AoE Slow — Dust Plague aura
decks.notes: Aura-control variant. Dust Plague (1s+2m) is the centerpiece — a 12s aura that ticks slow/stun automatically while granting armor, ideal for the multi-loop grind where it covers several enemies. Quake x2 + Frostbind + Tremor Lock provide on-demand control around it. Sandfury is the damage outlet converting all that slow. Order: cheap mana control first, Dust Plague mid (after mana banks for its 2m), Sandfury last as the closer. Note Dust Plague's peakOPS is very low in isolation — this deck is partly a stress test for whether the aura is too weak as a standalone slot.

decks.label: Boss2 Mage Control vs Iron Golem — Tectonic finisher
decks.notes: Iron golem (def-affinity armor ratchet) at loop20. Control buys time: Quake + Frostbind + Tremor Lock freeze the golem's attack cd while flat damage (mage INT still low ~10) chips through its armor. Dust Plague maintains passive slow/stun. The deck is built around Tectonic Reckoning as the exhaust nuke finisher (3s+2m, 3 stun + 50 pierce) placed LAST — it needs the full stamina+mana pool banked, which the long control phase provides. Sandfury is the secondary pierce outlet vs the golem's armor. This stresses whether pierce-based control can beat the armor-ratchet boss.

decks.label: Boss3 Mage Lockdown vs Lizard King
decks.notes: Loop30 boss. 12-card deck, mostly T3 control payoffs with cheap T1/T2 staples up front. Quake/Frostbind/Tremor Lock open the lock; Concussive Smash (2s) adds a stun+slow burst spike mid-deck; Cinder Squall + Sandfury convert slow to damage; Tectonic Reckoning is the exhaust closer. Dust Plague sustains the aura. Order keeps the single-cost control first and the multi-resource T3 payoffs late so they don't skip on empty pools. Tests control-archetype scaling into a higher-multiplier boss with low mage INT.

decks.label: Boss4 Mage Control vs Bog Witch — full lockdown
decks.notes: Loop40, bossMult 1.15. Deck doubles down on the stun chain: Frostbind -> Tremor Lock -> Bedrock Snare each escalate stun/slow only when the prior landed, so they're ordered Frostbind->Tremor Lock->Bedrock Snare. Cinder Squall + Sandfury are the slow-to-damage engine; Concussive Smash provides a stun spike; Tectonic Reckoning is the exhaust nuke finisher. Dust Plague holds the aura. With mage INT still modest at L15, the win condition is freezing the witch nearly perpetually while pierce damage accrues. Front-loaded cheap control ensures a reliable open.

decks.label: Boss5 Mage Control vs Desert Golem
decks.notes: Loop50, bossMult 1.2. Endgame-tuned 12-card control deck. Quake/Frostbind/Tremor Lock open; the back half is all T3: Cinder Squall and Sandfury convert slow to pierce, Concussive Smash spikes stun, Thunderstrike Catalyst detonates accumulated slow for burst, and Tectonic Reckoning is the exhaust finisher. Against a high-armor golem the pierce on Sandfury/Thunderstrike/Tectonic is the answer. Single-resource control leads, multi-resource T3 trails to avoid skips. This is near the ceiling of what slow/stun control can carry at low-INT mage power.

decks.label: Boss6 Mage Control vs Infernal Dragon — race-the-clock lock
decks.notes: Loop60, bossMult 1.25 — the toughest stage in this lane. Pure-control C3 archetypes are intentionally race-losing, so this deck is a realism stress test: can perpetual slow/stun (Quake, Frostbind, Tremor Lock, Bedrock Snare, Dust Plague aura) freeze the dragon long enough for the pierce win-cons (Sandfury, Thunderstrike Catalyst, Concussive Smash, and the Tectonic Reckoning nuke) to close before the timeout? Expect this to be near or below the win threshold — if it times out, it diagnoses that the control archetype lacks a strong enough damage outlet at endgame for a low-INT mage, pointing at buffing the slow-detonator (Thunderstrike Catalyst) or Cinder Squall's damage ceiling.

decks.label: Brineward sustain vs doom_knight
decks.notes: Loop-10 heal/poison mage. Opens cheap+reliable: Mend (1m), Misting Veil (1m, refunds mana + ramps int), Quake slow (1m) to throttle doom_knight's 9-dmg hits, then Steaming Plague (1s+1m) starts the poison clock early. Second Mend keeps the engine afloat while big T3 sustain (Brineward 14armor+heal12+poison, Tidesong heal18+mana) lands. Bog Catalyst doubles the poison applied by Steaming Plague/Brineward so the quadratic DoT outpaces the 380HP boss without needing INT. Order interleaves 1m cards with the 2m heals so mana never bottoms out.

decks.label: Phoenix-aura armor/heal vs doom_knight
decks.notes: Defensive variant leaning on the Phoenix Aura low-HP armor trigger as a second layer. Cheap open: Mend, Quake (slow), Vow of the Tide (armor+heal+stam). Alchemic Drain (1m, poison+heal6) is the efficient poison-and-sustain engine. Phoenix Aura grants 18 armor whenever below 50% HP — combined with Brineward's flat 14 armor + heal 12 this builds a wall the doom_knight can't break while poison (Drain + Brineward + Bog Catalyst double) closes it. Tighter 9-card list so it draws its payoffs reliably under fixed order.

decks.label: Tidesong poison-race (loop15)
decks.notes: Loop-15 (1.1 normal mult) heal/poison engine, 12 cards. Front half is the cheap mana-positive opener (Mend, Misting Veil refund, Quake slow, Steaming Plague + Alchemic Drain poison) so it never stalls; back half is the heavy T3 sustain + poison spike. Slipvenom Tempo (5 poison, +5 if 10+) capitalizes on the poison already laid by Steaming Plague/Bog Catalyst for a big quadratic jump. Brine Bedrock and Brineward sandwich heals between poison applications. Misted/Tidesong mana refunds keep the four 2m heals castable in sequence.

decks.label: Stall-and-control sustain (loop15)
decks.notes: Control-leaning sub-variant: Quake (slow) and Frostbind (stun+armor) open to blunt incoming damage while cheap Mend/Misting Veil keep HP topped. Misted Cadence + Tidesong both refund 3 mana so the deck can chain big heals against the higher loop-15 enemy multiplier. Poison kill via Steaming Plague + Bog Catalyst double + Alchemic Drain. Slightly more defensive than loop15-1; trades a poison spike (Slipvenom) for Frostbind control. Cheap cards front-loaded so the slow/stun lock lands before the first big enemy swings.

decks.label: Poison-through-armor sustain vs iron_golem
decks.notes: iron_golem stacks armor (defense affinity), so the plan is DoT-first: poison (quadratic, applied as stacks) is far less blunted by the armor ratchet than flat magic hits. Steaming Plague leads the poison, Bog Catalyst doubles it, Slipvenom Tempo spikes it past 10 stacks. Heals (Brineward, Tidesong, Brine Bedrock, Phoenix Aura) keep the mage alive through the long race the golem forces. Cheap Mend/Misting Veil open and refund mana; the two-mana heals are spaced by 1m poison/refund cards so mana stays solvent for the full grind.

decks.label: Brineward wall + poison vs iron_golem
decks.notes: Armor+heal double-wall variant for the golem fight. Vow of the Tide and Brineward stack flat armor on top of heals so the golem's per-hit damage is soaked, while Phoenix Aura adds 18 armor at low HP as a safety net. Quake slow throttles the golem's attack cadence. Poison engine (Steaming Plague + Alchemic Drain + Bog Catalyst double) does the killing since it ignores the armor race. Misted Cadence + Tidesong refund mana to sustain the heal chain. Cheap opener guarantees the wall is up before the golem ramps.

decks.label: Endgame poison-sustain vs lizard_king
decks.notes: Loop-30 tuned heal/poison list (13 cards, mostly T3 with cheap T1 mana glue). Win condition is a saturated poison clock: Steaming Plague -> Bog Catalyst double -> Slipvenom Tempo (+5 at 10 stacks) builds a huge quadratic DoT early, then four big heals (Tidesong 18, Phoenix 15, Brine Bedrock 15, Misted 14, Brineward 12) plus Brineward's 14 armor outlast lizard_king's damage. Three mana-refund heals (Tidesong, Misted, Squall via mana) bankroll the 2m spells. Mend x2 + Misting Veil are the early-slot tempo so the engine opens every run under fixed order.

decks.label: Fortress heal/control vs lizard_king
decks.notes: Maximum-survival endgame variant: Quake + Frostbind open the control, then a heal/armor fortress (Brineward 14 armor+heal12, Phoenix Aura 18 armor at low HP, Standing Stone 16 armor + Brace heal 8) stacks mitigation while poison (Steaming Plague + Alchemic Drain + Bog Catalyst double) does the slow kill. Tidesong + Misted Cadence refund 3 mana each to keep the wall standing through lizard_king's long fight. Trades some poison spike for extra armor layers vs the harder boss; cheap cards front-loaded so control and the first heals land before the boss ramps.

decks.label: Brineward Outlast (boss2)
decks.notes: Open Mireglide (1m armor+poison+haste) and Tidefoot Guard (1m 12armor+poison) to survive iron_golem's 10dmg/2.5s while seeding poison; Mire Bloom (1m) is cheap filler poison; Bog Catalyst (1s+1m) doubles the stack toward the chunk cap; Stagnant Bulwark (1s+1m) lays a poison-over-time field behind 10 armor; Brineward (2m 14armor+heal12+poison) anchors the outlast; Drowning Lance detonates half the pool for pierce; Brine Bedrock & Mistplate top up armor/heal/poison. Cheap 1m cards front-loaded so the deck always opens; only two 2m cards and they trail so mana has regenerated. SIM (6 reps, iron_golem bossMult1.05, eHP409): winRate 1.0, hp 1.0, ttk 20s, 0 deaths/timeouts.

decks.label: Mistplate Slow-Wall (boss2)
decks.notes: Adds slow throttle to the armor wall. Stormsplash (1m poison+slow+burn) and Mistplate (2m armor+poison+slow) cut the golem's attack cadence while poison ticks; Bog Catalyst doubles; Marsh Squall is the AoE poison detonator; Bogplate (1s+1m vengeance poison) and Brineward backstop armor+heal. Order keeps all 1m appliers up front before the 2m anchors. SIM (6 reps vs iron_golem): winRate 1.0, hp 1.0, ttk 21s, 12 cards cycled, 0 deaths/timeouts.

decks.label: Bog Catalyst Stackwall (boss3)
decks.notes: Vs lizard_king (enrages <30% HP, multi-hit) race poison to the cap and detonate before enrage. Mireglide + Tidefoot Guard open armor+poison; Alchemic Drain (1m poison+heal6) is cheap sustain; Bog Catalyst doubles the stack; Stagnant Bulwark drips poison behind armor; Drowning Lance is the big detonator; Brineward/Brine Bedrock/Mistplate/Bogplate keep the wall up. Detonated poison (pierce) bypasses def 3 and closes the fight before enrage matters. SIM (6 reps, lizard_king bossMult1.1, eHP388): winRate 1.0, hp 1.0, ttk 15s, 0 deaths/timeouts.

decks.label: Steam Bulwark Brace (boss3)
decks.notes: Poison+burn+block hybrid. Boilstep (1m burn+poison) and Steam Bulwark (2m armor + brace poison+burn) stack a burn layer alongside poison so both DoTs tick; Bog Catalyst doubles poison; Drowning Lance detonates. Cheap 1m appliers lead; the two 2m walls (Brineward, Steam Bulwark) plus Bogplate sit after mana has banked. SIM (6 reps vs lizard_king): winRate 1.0, hp 0.99, ttk 16s, 0 deaths/timeouts.

decks.label: Drain Wall vs Bog Witch (boss4)
decks.notes: Bog_witch drains 15% and only hits for 7, so the danger is the timeout not death. Poison ignores armor and can't be drained back, so this maxes poison throughput: Mireglide/Tidefoot open, Alchemic Drain cheap sustain, Bog Catalyst doubles, Stagnant Bulwark drips, then Drowning Lance + Marsh Squall give TWO detonators so the chunk-capped pool converts to burst pierce and actually closes the fight. Brineward/Brine Bedrock heal through the witch's chip. SIM (6 reps, bog_witch bossMult1.15, eHP440): winRate 1.0, hp 1.0, ttk 18s, dmg 501, 0 deaths/timeouts.

decks.label: Twin-Detonator Race (boss4)
decks.notes: Leaner, more aggressive poison build to beat the witch's heal-race. Slipstream (1m pierce+poison) and Venom Dance (1s 3/+5 poison on vengeance) ramp the stack fast; Bog Catalyst doubles; Drowning Lance + Marsh Squall are the two detonators; Stagnant Bulwark and Brineward provide the only armor/heal so it must close quickly. Front-loaded 1m/1s appliers keep tempo high. SIM (6 reps vs bog_witch): winRate 1.0, hp 1.0, ttk 21s, 14 cards cycled, 0 deaths/timeouts.

decks.label: Endgame Poison Fortress (boss5)
decks.notes: Tuned vs desert_golem (450 HP, def 4, 10dmg/2.7s, +10 shield/7s). Two openers of cheap armor+poison (Mireglide, Tidefoot Guard), Alchemic Drain sustain, Bog Catalyst + Slipvenom Tempo to spike and re-spike the stack past 10, Stagnant Bulwark drip, then Drowning Lance + Marsh Squall detonators to punch through def/shield with pierce. Brineward/Brine Bedrock heal the long fight. Order keeps all 1m appliers ahead of the 2m walls. SIM (6 reps, desert_golem bossMult1.2, eHP495): winRate 1.0, hp 1.0, ttk 18s, dmg 522, 0 deaths/timeouts.

decks.label: Shield-Pierce Poison (boss5)
decks.notes: Maximizes pierce-detonation against desert_golem's def 4 + periodic shield, which blunts ordinary armor-based damage. Slipvenom Tempo big poison spike, Bog Catalyst doubler, then Drowning Lance + Marsh Squall convert the capped pool to pierce that ignores def/shield. Magma Welling and Stagnant Bulwark/Brineward/Mistplate hold the wall. 1m appliers lead, 2m anchors trail. SIM (6 reps vs desert_golem): winRate 1.0, hp 1.0, ttk 13s, dmg 528, 0 deaths/timeouts.

decks.label: Slow-detonator control vs iron_golem (Thunderstrike line)
decks.notes: Slow-control opener: cheap Quake (1m) then Stormsplash (1m, poison+slow+burn) and Cinder Squall (1s+1m, AoE slow that converts to stun + slow-scaled damage) build the slow pool every loop. Gale Echo (1s+1m) doubles every slow applied so the pool climbs fast. Thunderstrike Catalyst sits mid-deck as the slow detonator (consume all slow, 6 per slow Pierce) once 4-5 stacks exist. Quake/Spark/Stormsplash are 1-cost front-loaded so the deck reliably opens on a near-empty pool; the 1s+1m 'both' cards are interleaved so neither mana nor stamina starves. Stormsplash poison and Slipvenom add a second clock vs iron_golem's armor-affinity ratchet. Concussive Smash closes if the detonator under-delivers.

decks.label: Poison-detonator control vs iron_golem (Marsh Squall line)
decks.notes: Poison-stacking detonator: Stormsplash (1m) and Alchemic Drain (1m) open cheaply and seed poison; Bog Catalyst (1s+1m) doubles the poison pool; Slipvenom Tempo (1s+1m) and Steaming Plague (1s+1m) pile on volume since low mage INT means we lean on flat stacks. Marsh Squall (2s, exhaust) is the detonator — placed mid-deck after the doublers so it consumes a fat pool for 6 Pierce per poison AoE. Cinder Squall adds slow→stun control to survive iron_golem's hits while poison ticks. Cheap 1-cost Spark/Quake/Alchemic Drain front-loaded keeps the mana-heavy deck flowing under +1m/4.5s regen.

decks.label: Burn-detonator control vs lizard_king (Supernova line)
decks.notes: Burn bank-and-cash into Supernova: Spark (1m), Flame Dart (1m), Pyre (1m) front-load cheap burn application so the pool banks every loop. Pyre Surge (1s+1m) doubles the burn pool and adds +2/burn before the detonator. Supernova (2s+1m, exhaust) is the payoff — 5 INT-scaled Pierce per burn consumed to all enemies, placed after the doublers. Cinder Squall + Gale Echo + Dust Plague layer slow/stun control so the mage survives lizard_king's tempo while burn banks; Venom Detonation gives a second smaller detonate. Cheap 1m cards up front guarantee the open; 'both' cards interleaved to avoid mana stall.

decks.label: Poison/burn dual-detonator control vs lizard_king
decks.notes: Dual-DoT detonator: Stormsplash (1m), Boilstep (1m, poison+burn), Slipvenom (1s+1m), Steaming Plague (1s+1m) build both poison and burn pools cheaply up front. Venom Detonation (1s+1m) cashes the burn pool inline; Marsh Squall (2s, exhaust) cashes the bigger poison pool as AoE Pierce. Bog Catalyst doubles poison before the detonate. Gale Echo + Cinder Squall maintain slow/stun control so the mage buys time while two DoT clocks run. Front-loaded 1m appliers keep the open reliable; the 'both' cards are spaced so stamina (30) isn't drained by the back-to-back detonators.

decks.label: Poison-detonator control vs bog_witch (Marsh Squall, scaled)
decks.notes: Deep poison-detonator for L15. Heavy front of cheap appliers — Stormsplash (1m), Boilstep (1m), Slipvenom (1s+1m), Steaming Plague (1s+1m), Venom Dance (1s) — to outpace bog_witch's own poison/heal. TWO Bog Catalyst copies double the pool aggressively; Marsh Squall (2s, exhaust) detonates for huge AoE Pierce. Cinder Squall + Gale Echo + Dust Plague form the slow→stun control shell to freeze bog_witch's casts while poison ticks. Mostly T3 with no dead T2; mana-leaning with 1s/1m splashes front-loaded so the deck never opens empty under the slow regen.

decks.label: Slow-lock control + Thunderstrike vs bog_witch
decks.notes: Slow-lock variant: Quake (1m) and Stormsplash (1m) open, Cinder Squall + Gale Echo + Dust Plague stack slow every loop and convert overflow to stun (Gale Echo doubles each slow applied, Dust Plague tickers slow every 2.5s). Thunderstrike Catalyst is the slow detonator (6 Pierce per slow). Tremor Lock + Concussive Smash + Tectonic Reckoning (3s+2m, exhaust nuke) punish a stunned bog_witch with big hits. Slipvenom + Bog Catalyst + Steaming Plague provide a poison backup clock since pure slow has low raw damage at low INT. Cheap 1-cost openers guarantee the early slots fire.

decks.label: Poison-detonator control vs infernal_dragon (endgame poison)
decks.notes: Endgame (L18) poison-detonator. Front of cheap appliers — Stormsplash (1m), Boilstep (1m), Slipvenom (1s+1m), Steaming Plague (1s+1m), Venom Dance (1s) — generates a huge poison pool fast; two Bog Catalyst doublers compound it; Marsh Squall (2s, exhaust) detonates as 6 Pierce/poison AoE. Cinder Squall + Gale Echo + Dust Plague + Thunderstrike form a slow/stun shell to survive the dragon's heavy burn pressure. Pure-mana economy backed by 1s splashes; detonator + Thunderstrike are the only stamina sinks so the 30 pool sustains. Tests whether poison-detonate scales into the 1.25x boss / 1.5x normal band.

decks.label: Burn Supernova + control vs infernal_dragon
decks.notes: Burn-detonate at L18. Spark/Flame Dart/Pyre (all 1m) front-load burn banking; Pyre Surge (1s+1m) doubles the pool +2/burn; Venom Detonation cashes a smaller burn pool inline; Supernova (2s+1m, exhaust) is the big AoE burn detonator. Cinder Squall + Gale Echo + Dust Plague hold slow/stun control. A poison sub-engine (Stormsplash, Bog Catalyst, Slipvenom) gives a second clock because the dragon's HP is high enough that one burn dump may not close. Mana-heavy with the two exhaust 'both' detonators spaced apart so stamina recovers between them.

decks.label: All-DoT triple-detonator vs boss_iron_golem (final)
decks.notes: Final-boss (L19) all-DoT build vs the armor-affinity boss_iron_golem. Cheap appliers up front (Stormsplash 1m, Boilstep 1m, Slipvenom 1s+1m, Steaming Plague 1s+1m) seed poison+burn; two Bog Catalyst doublers compound poison; Venom Detonation cashes burn, Marsh Squall (2s) and Tremor Detonate (2s, exhaust) cash poison/burn/bleed as Pierce — Pierce matters because boss_iron_golem ratchets armor. Cinder Squall + Gale Echo + Dust Plague + Thunderstrike are the slow/stun control shell. Three detonators are spread across the deck so the 30 stamina pool can recover the 2s costs between casts.

decks.label: Slow-lock Thunderstrike + Tectonic finisher vs boss_iron_golem
decks.notes: Slow-lock finisher for the final boss. Quake (1m) + Stormsplash (1m) open; Cinder Squall + Gale Echo + Dust Plague stack slow and convert to stun (Gale Echo doubles each application, Dust Plague tickers it). Thunderstrike Catalyst detonates slow for 6 Pierce/stack; Tremor Lock + Concussive Smash hit a stunned target; Tectonic Reckoning (3s+2m, exhaust) is the all-or-nothing Pierce nuke that ignores the golem's armor ratchet while it's stun-locked. Poison backup (Slipvenom, Bog Catalyst, Steaming Plague, Venom Dance) supplies steady damage since slow itself deals none. Cheap 1m openers + spaced stamina sinks keep both pools alive against boss_iron_golem's 1.3x scaling.

decks.label: Loop8 Casual Burn Mess (no payoff)
decks.notes: Genuinely weak/messy loop-8 burn draft. Opens with cheap 1m Spark and free Gust (Gust is a dead card: pure haste, no damage), then Mend (heal = race-losing per C3). The burn 'payoff' Pyre sits in slot 7 and Magma Vein (2m, 2.3 CD) is buried last, so the deck applies burn faster than it ever cashes it; t2-air-fire (Firestorm, peakOPS 0 conditional aura) frequently whiffs. Quake (pure slow, no damage) is a dead slot. Low mage INT (~3) means the flat burn/spark numbers stay tiny. Curve is front-loadable but the win condition never assembles — should win normal loop-8 enemies only slowly.

decks.label: Loop8 Control-No-Kill (slow/stun, no win con)
decks.notes: A casual 'control' pile that can't actually kill. Quake (pure slow, no damage per C5) and Gust (free, dead) lead; the two payoff cards Tremor Lock and Bedrock Snare are both gated on the enemy already being stunned, which this deck rarely achieves, so they skip/whiff. Frostbind gives a little stun+armor but no scaling. Heal (Mend) and Misting Veil (peakOPS 2.57) are wasted tempo. Only real damage is one buried Spark dealing ~2. Demonstrates a weak mage with near-zero offense; likely timeouts on normals.

decks.label: Loop8 Mana-Hungry Stall (bad curve)
decks.notes: Curve-sabotage variant: leads with Magma Vein (2m, 2.3s CD) and Pyre back-to-back, two same-resource mana sinks that out-pace the +1 mana/4.5s regen, so the deck stalls and skips in the opening. Healing and Misting Veil dilute further. The cheap reliable cards (Spark, Flame Dart) that should be up front are buried at the back. Intentionally shows how a same-resource, expensive-first mana deck stalls out at loop 8.

decks.label: Boss1 Weak Burn Mage vs doom_knight (easy-check)
decks.notes: The headline doom_knight easy-check for a WEAK mage. Opens with cheap reliable burn (Spark, Flame Dart) then the Pyre detonator at slot 4, which is reasonable, but the deck is still soft: Gust (dead free card), Mend (heal, race-losing), Quake (pure slow no damage), and a slow 2.3s Magma Vein dilute the clock. Against doom_knight's 380 HP / def 3 / 9 dmg, low INT (~3) keeps burn payoffs small (Pyre deals 4 + ~3/burn) so the race is tight; this is the deck that tests whether an 'easy' first boss is genuinely beatable by a mediocre mage or punishingly slow.

decks.label: Boss1 Overhealer Mage (race-loser)
decks.notes: A casual 'I'll just outlast it' mage that violates the C3 race-loser principle in practice: three healing/defensive cards (Mend, Steam Surge heal mode, Misting Veil, Frostbind) up front, only thin burn for offense. Against doom_knight (9 dmg, 380 HP) the heals buy time but the deck lacks the damage to ever close, so it should trend toward timeouts/long TTK — a clean demonstration that defense without a second win condition can't beat even the easy first boss as a mage.

decks.label: Boss1 Splashy Off-Element Mage (incoherent)
decks.notes: Incoherent 'rainbow' draft a real casual player ends up with: dabbles in poison (Mire Bloom), burn (Spark, Pyre), and control (Bedrock Snare) without committing to any, plus DEX-scaled splashes (Gale Cut, Mist Step) that scale off the mage's low DEX. No archetype reaches critical mass, the lone poison source never doubles, and Pyre has almost no burn pool to consume. Front cards are cheap so it opens, but it has no real kill plan vs doom_knight — a representative weak/suboptimal mage that the easy-check should still ideally clear, slowly.

decks.label: Mixed elemental starter (loop5)
decks.notes: Fresh 6-card drafted toolbox. Opens with the two cheapest reliable 1m cards (Spark burn+dmg, Mend heal cushion) so the deck never stalls on empty mana. t2-fire-water (Steam Surge) is the workhorse: 6 dmg +4 vs burn, heal 4, refunds 1 stamina. t2-earth-water (Mire Bloom) is the perfect toolbox card: armor+heal+dmg+poison in one. t2-air-air (Tailwind) deals dmg, hastes, REFUNDS 1 mana to keep the cheap engine spinning. t1-earth (Quake slow) closes as soft control. Avg ~1m cost is within passive regen + Tailwind refund. INT~5 so flat values carry.

decks.label: Mixed toolbox w/ tempo splash (loop5)
decks.notes: Variant leaning on free/cheap tempo. t1-air (free Gust haste) opens for nothing so something always plays turn 1, then Spark (1m). t1-attack (Jab, 9 dmg, 1s) is a cheap stamina splash giving reliable on-curve damage while mana banks. Steam Surge + Mire Bloom are the dual-element core. t1-water heal sits mid-deck as the cushion. t2-agility-fire (Flame Dart, 1m, 5 dmg + 2 burn) closes. Interleaves stamina/mana/free so no two same-resource expensive cards stack back to back.

decks.label: Mixed elemental toolbox (loop8)
decks.notes: 9-card deck adding the first T3. Cheap Spark opener, then the Steam Surge/Mire Bloom/Tailwind cheap-mana core (Tailwind refunds mana to sustain). Flame Dart + Pyre (t2-fire-fire, burn detonate) build and cash burn. Quake adds slow. Closes with t3-earth-fire-water (Alchemic Drain, only 1m): poison + heal, an efficient cheap T3 that fits the toolbox. Front-loaded cheap cards keep it opening reliably; one true T3 at this stage is realistic.

decks.label: Mixed toolbox, poison-leaning (loop8)
decks.notes: 10-card variant tilting the toolbox toward poison+burn DoT while keeping heal/slow. Opens Spark+Mend, then Mire Bloom (poison) and Steam Surge (burn payoff). Tailwind sustains mana. Two cheap 1m T3s: Alchemic Drain (poison+heal) and Boilstep (t3-agility-fire-water: 6 Pierce + burn + poison). Quake/Flame Dart/Jab round out control + cheap filler at the back so the deck never bricks. Sustainable avg cost.

decks.label: Mixed toolbox vs doom_knight (boss1)
decks.notes: Loop-10 average drafted mage vs doom_knight (380 HP, def 3, 9 dmg). Cheap Spark opener, then the Steam Surge/Mire Bloom/Tailwind core for dmg+armor+heal+mana sustain. Burn line (Pyre, Flame Dart) plus poison T3s: Alchemic Drain (poison+heal), Boilstep (burn+poison), and t3-air-fire-water (Steaming Plague: 3->5 poison) for the unblockable kill. Mend + Mire Bloom + heals buy HP cushion the boss demands; Quake slows its hits. Beatable-but-not-trivial profile: DoT closes while heal/slow survive its 9-dmg swings.

decks.label: Mixed toolbox boss1 (control tilt)
decks.notes: Loop-10 average drafted mage vs doom_knight. Opens Spark + Mend for the HP cushion the boss demands, then Mire Bloom + Steam Surge for armor/heal/dmg. Tailwind sustains mana. Adds slow/stun control vs the boss's hard hits: t2-earth-earth, Quake, and t3-air-air-fire (Cinder Squall: slow->stun + dmg). t3-earth-earth-water (Bog Catalyst) doubles poison for the kill; t3-agility-air-water (Stormsplash) layers poison+slow+burn. Heal + slow buy time for the poison/burn DoT to close — the toolbox plan vs a beatable boss.

decks.label: Mixed elemental toolbox (loop15)
decks.notes: 13-card mid deck, mostly T3 with cheap T2 staples. Spark opener; Steam Surge/Mire Bloom/Tailwind cheap core sustains mana. Poison engine: Steaming Plague (3->5 poison), Bog Catalyst (double poison), Alchemic Drain + Boilstep (cheap poison+burn). Heal from Mend/Mire Bloom, slow/stun from Cinder Squall. t3-fire-fire-water (Quench Lance) ramps INT for the DoT. Cheap cards front and back so the deck opens and never bricks on mana; expensive 2m cards are absent to stay sustainable.

decks.label: Mixed toolbox vs iron_golem (boss2)
decks.notes: vs iron_golem (armor-ratchet boss). Poison ignores the armor affinity, so the toolbox tilts poison-heavy: Steaming Plague + Bog Catalyst (double) + Boilstep + Alchemic Drain stack DoT that bypasses the golem's defense. Cheap Spark/Mire Bloom/Steam Surge/Tailwind open and sustain. Slow/stun (earth-earth, Cinder Squall, Quake) throttle the golem's hits. t3-defense-water-water (Brineward) is the survival anchor: armor+heal+poison. Heal/slow buy time for the unblockable DoT to win.

decks.label: Mixed toolbox endgame (boss5)
decks.notes: 15-card forge-cap toolbox vs desert_golem (boss5, mult 1.2). Mostly T3. Poison is the unblockable backbone (Steaming Plague, Bog Catalyst double, Boilstep, Stormsplash, Alchemic Drain) with Quench Lance ramping INT to amplify every stack. Survival from t3-fire-water-water (Phoenix Aura: heal+burn+emergency armor) and t3-defense-water-water (Brineward: armor+heal+poison). Cheap Spark/Mire Bloom/Steam Surge/Tailwind/Mend open and sustain mana so the 1m / 1s+1m T3s never brick. Cinder Squall + Quake throttle the golem. Wins by out-DoTing through armor while heals outlast its damage.

decks.label: Mixed toolbox burn+poison (boss5)
